evidence: goseg/uploadsvc/service.go defines `OpenEndpointRequest` but also imports `groundseg/structs` and provides `OpenEndpointRequestFromWsPayload(payload structs.WsUploadPayload)`, coupling the service package to websocket transport DTOs.

goseg/handler/upload.go depends on upload action constants from `uploadsvc`, then converts payload into service request through `uploadsvc.OpenEndpointRequestFromWsPayload`, so transport-to-domain mapping is split across boundary layers.

goseg/uploadsvc/service.go co-locates the service interface (`Service`) with the concrete importer adapter (`ImporterService`) that directly calls `importer.OpenUploadEndpoint` and `importer.Reset`, blending boundary contract and infrastructure implementation.

README mandates explicit upload branch-matrix testing (decode failure, open-endpoint success/failure, reset success/failure, unsupported action), but runtime logic for these branches is encoded in `goseg/handler/upload.go` without a machine-enforced link to `uploadsvc` action definitions.

README’s documented CI policy (`Upload Contract Gate`) only describes path-aware coupling for `goseg/handler/upload.go` and `goseg/handler/upload_test.go`, leaving `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` action/translation contract and `goseg/startram/errors.go` masking contract outside that gate.

README requires masked-error tests asserting redaction and preserved cause chain; `goseg/startram/errors.go` defines the wrapper contract but no governance mechanism in these files enforces that requirement.

Workflow handlers repeatedly combine domain operations with UI transition publishing and sleep-based state clearing (e.g., `goseg/handler/newship.go`, `goseg/handler/urbit_operations.go`, `goseg/handler/startram.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `goseg/handler/support.go`).

Drive-selection and mountpoint validation logic is reimplemented across onboarding/import paths with slightly different behavior (`goseg/handler/handler.go`, `goseg/handler/newship.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`).
Cleanup/rollback flows for failed ship creation/import are duplicated instead of centrally orchestrated (`goseg/handler/newship.go:errorCleanup`, `goseg/importer/importer.go:errorCleanup`).

Some modules still act as broad orchestration hubs instead of focused components (`goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go` builds multiple domain views and mutates shared global state; `goseg/system/wifi.go` mixes AP control, websocket transport, and wifi polling with package-level mutable state).

findings.summary: Upload service layer is directly coupled to websocket payload structs.
evidence: `uploadsvc.OpenEndpointRequestFromWsPayload` accepts `structs.WsUploadPayload`, forcing service code to import transport-layer types.

Handler routing in `UploadMessageHandler.Handle` switches transport action strings and then delegates transport-to-domain conversion to `uploadsvc`, so boundary translation responsibility is not isolated at the edge.
findings.suggestion: Move `OpenEndpointRequestFromWsPayload` out of `uploadsvc` into `handler` (or a dedicated transport adapter package). Keep `uploadsvc` accepting only `OpenEndpointRequest` and domain-level types so websocket schema changes do not force service-package edits.

findings.summary: Service contract and concrete importer adapter are merged in one package/file.
evidence: `uploadsvc` exports `Service` but also ships `ImporterService` that directly invokes importer package functions, so the same package owns both abstraction and infrastructure detail.
`handler/upload.go` depends on this mixed package for both action contract (`ActionOpenEndpoint`, `ActionReset`) and runtime behavior (`service.OpenEndpoint`/`Reset`).

`startram/errors.go` shows a separate package-specific boundary wrapper (`wrapAPIConnectionError`) while upload path lacks an equivalent boundary-specific adapter layer, indicating inconsistent boundary ownership across subsystems.
findings.suggestion: Split upload service contract/types from importer-backed implementation: keep `uploadsvc` as contract-only (interfaces, request types, actions), move `ImporterService` to a separate adapter package (for example `uploadsvc/importeradapter`), and wire it at composition/root level.

findings.summary: Upload action contract is split between service constants and handler switch logic.
evidence: `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` exports `SupportedActions()` and typed action constants, but `goseg/handler/upload.go` separately encodes routing behavior in its own `switch` over `uploadPayload.Payload.Action`.
Adding a new action requires parallel edits in `SupportedActions`, handler `switch`, and README’s required test matrix, which increases boundary drift risk.
findings.suggestion: Move action dispatch into `uploadsvc` (for example, a single `Execute(action, payload)` or action-handler map keyed by `Action`) so handler code only decodes transport input and forwards once; derive supported-actions from the same dispatch table.

findings.summary: Upload service contract and importer adapter are co-located, weakening boundary integrity.
evidence: `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` defines `Service` and `OpenEndpointRequest` but also concrete `ImporterService` that imports and calls `groundseg/importer` directly.
This arrangement makes the contract package transitively depend on importer infrastructure, reducing modular replaceability for compatibility/policy variants.
findings.suggestion: Split `uploadsvc` into a pure contract layer (actions, request DTO, interface) and a separate adapter implementation file/package for importer-backed behavior; wire concrete implementation in composition root.

findings.summary: Documented quality gates do not cover all runtime contracts they claim to enforce.
evidence: README requires upload routing matrix tests and masked-error contract tests, but its explicit CI gate description only enforces handler file/test coupling (`goseg/handler/upload.go` vs `goseg/handler/upload_test.go`).

Critical contract code exists outside that gate: `OpenEndpointRequestFromWsPayload` mapping and action definitions in `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go`, plus masking wrapper behavior in `goseg/startram/errors.go`.
findings.suggestion: Extend quality gates so contract changes in `goseg/uploadsvc/service.go` and `goseg/startram/errors.go` require corresponding targeted tests (including `errors.Is`/`Unwrap` assertions for masked errors and dispatch-table parity tests for upload actions).

findings.summary: Ship/startram/import workflows duplicate UI-transition choreography inside domain operations, creating a repeated mixed-responsibility pattern.
evidence: `createUrbitShip`, multiple urbit operation handlers, and startram handlers all publish transitions/events directly, perform core operations, then sleep and clear status in the same functions.
`runImportPhases` plus `errorCleanup` in importer ties lifecycle execution to websocket transition emission instead of separating orchestration from business actions.
`SupportHandler` interleaves bug-report file work, profiling, upload transport, and transition timing (`time.Sleep`) in one request path.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared operation-orchestrator abstraction (phase runner + standardized transition policy) and keep handlers focused on invoking domain use-cases; move sleep/clear semantics into one reusable transition policy component.

findings.summary: Custom-drive detection/formatting logic is duplicated across new-ship and import entry points with diverging control flow.
evidence: `NewShipHandler` and `createUrbitShip` both walk `ListHardDisks()` and apply `/groundseg-<n>` regex checks.
`OpenUploadEndpoint` in importer repeats the same mountpoint search but encodes result through `NeedsFormatting` and later formatting in `ensureUploadDriveReady`.
The same domain rule (resolve selected drive to usable mountpoint/format requirement) is implemented in multiple places with different error/formatting behavior.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single drive-resolution service returning `{mountpoint, needsFormatting, validationError}` and use it from both new-ship and importer workflows so formatting and error semantics stay consistent.

findings.summary: Failure rollback logic for ship provisioning is copied into separate cleanup routines, increasing drift risk.
evidence: `newship.errorCleanup` and `importer.errorCleanup` both remove configs, update system entries, and delete volumes with near-parallel structure.
Deletion/stop/recovery concerns also appear in `deleteShip`, `togglePower`, `rebuildContainer`, and `urbitCleanDelete` with overlapping but non-identical sequencing.
Rollback behavior is encoded per workflow rather than as reusable compensating actions.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared ship-lifecycle compensation module (idempotent steps for config removal, container teardown, volume/custom-path cleanup, transition emission) and compose it from workflows instead of duplicating procedural cleanup blocks.

findings.summary: Broadcast package combines snapshot building, domain aggregation, transport emission, and global state mutation in one module.
evidence: `broadcast.go` constructs urbit/profile/apps/system views, mutates module globals, queries docker/system/config, and emits websocket updates in the same package.
`runtimeSnapshotForPiers` mutates `docker.ContainerStatList` as a side effect while assembling broadcast snapshots.
`constructSystemInfo` and `assembleUrbitView` are long aggregation functions with cross-domain reads and formatting rules embedded directly.
findings.suggestion: Split broadcast into focused collaborators: snapshot collectors (system/docker/startram), pure view mappers, and a thin publisher; remove side-effectful cross-package mutation from snapshot assembly paths.

findings.summary: WiFi module mixes device control, AP lifecycle, websocket API handling, and shared mutable state without a clear boundary.
evidence: `wifi.go` holds websocket upgrader/client map, captive portal config, wifi polling loop, and nmcli/systemctl command execution in one package-level module.
Global mutable structures (`clients`, `WifiInfo`, `Device`, `ConfChannel`) are read/written across goroutines with partial locking only for C2C mode flag.
Handler layer delegates directly to this broad module (`C2CHandler`), reinforcing a coarse interface instead of a focused service boundary.
findings.suggestion: Split wifi responsibilities into separate components (radio control service, AP/captive service, websocket adapter) with explicit synchronization and narrow interfaces; keep handler-facing API at a use-case level.
